---
title: "Wildfire"
description: "Area burned across Canada each year, and the record 2023 fire season"
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import CANADA_COLOR, get_data_date
from pipeline.charts import bubble_map

MAP_CONFIG = {"displaylogo": False, "scrollZoom": True,
              "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]}
```

## Area burned each year

Wildfire is part of the boreal landscape, but the area burned varies enormously from year to year — and **2023 was, by a wide margin, the worst fire season on record**, with about **17.6 million hectares** burned across Canada (roughly the size of Florida). The chart shows the national total each year since 1959.

```{python}
#| echo: false
wf_path = "data/geography/nfdb_wildfire.csv"
if os.path.exists(wf_path):
    w = pd.read_csv(wf_path)
    ca = w[w["geography"] == "Canada"].sort_values("year").copy()
    ca["mha"] = ca["area_burned_ha"] / 1e6
    fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(
        x=ca["year"], y=ca["mha"], marker_color=CANADA_COLOR,
        hovertemplate="<b>%{x}</b><br>%{y:.1f} million ha burned<extra></extra>"))
    fig.update_layout(
        plot_bgcolor="white", height=420,
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        yaxis=dict(title="Million hectares burned", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        margin=dict(t=30, b=120),
        annotations=[dict(text=f"Source: Natural Resources Canada, Canadian National Fire Database | Data as of: {get_data_date(wf_path)}",
                          xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.18, showarrow=False,
                          font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "displayModeBar": False})
else:
    print("Wildfire data not yet available — run the data pipeline.")
```

## The record 2023 season, fire by fire

In that record 2023 season, the burning was concentrated in the boreal forest. The map below plots **every wildfire recorded that year as a single point, sized by the area it burned** — zoom and pan to your region. A handful of enormous fires in Quebec, the Northwest Territories and British Columbia account for most of the 17.6 million hectares, while thousands of smaller fires speckle the rest of the country. (Marker area is proportional to hectares burned, so the giant fires stand out from the many small ones.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
fp_path = "data/geo/nfdb_fire_points_2023.csv"
if os.path.exists(fp_path):
    fp = pd.read_csv(fp_path)
    fig = bubble_map(fp, "lat", "lon", "size_ha", color="#e3492a",
        customdata=fp[["province", "size_ha", "cause"]].to_numpy(),
        hovertemplate="<b>%{customdata[0]}</b><br>%{customdata[1]:,.0f} ha burned · %{customdata[2]}<extra></extra>",
        center={"lat": 60, "lon": -100}, zoom=2.3,
        source_note="Source: NRCan Canadian National Fire Database (NFDB), 2023 fire points; marker area ∝ hectares burned.")
    fig.show(config=MAP_CONFIG)
else:
    print("Wildfire-points data not yet available — run `python -m pipeline.build_geography`.")
```

Wildfire smoke is also the main reason fine-particulate air pollution has stopped improving in Canada — see [Air Quality](../environment/air-quality.qmd).

## Download data

- [Wildfire area burned, national & by province (CSV)](../data/geography/nfdb_wildfire.csv) — NRCan Canadian National Fire Database
